title: Add user auth, admin dashboard, and outgoing webhooks
summary: The panel was unusually accurate — almost everything reflects something real in this diff, so the work here was mostly MERGING duplicate flags rather than pruning false positives. Heavy overlap: kiss_zealot and quality_critic independently flagged the same security set (committed .env secrets, MD5 hashing, admin-master-key backdoor, ?adminBypass=1, eval-based user store, new Function() RCE, PAN/CVC storage+logging, plaintext password log, password_hash in response, reset-token leak, requireAdmin duplication) — these are the high-confidence core and are kept as `must`. I merged the userStore SQL-parser/eval KISS complaint with the eval code-injection security flag (same code), folded perf_skeptic's 'new Function per row' into the adminService RCE finding (the fix is to delete it), and merged webhook URL validation into the SSRF finding. I demoted speculative, in-memory-only perf items (findAll+filter, CSV buffering, serial delivery) to `suggestion` and kept honest `should` severities for defense-in-depth items (JWT expiry, CSPRNG, CORS, user enumeration, body-limit, validation). Nothing was a clear enough false positive to drop entirely.

findings.title: Production JWT secret, admin API key, and live payment key committed to git
findings.message: JWT_SECRET (`...prod-2026`), ADMIN_API_KEY (`hb_admin_...`), and PAYMENT_GATEWAY_LIVE_KEY (`pg_live_...`) are real-looking production credentials committed in plaintext, and .gitignore was patched with `!.env` to force-include them. Once in history they are permanent. Anyone with the repo can forge admin JWTs and call the live gateway. Rotate/revoke all three immediately, remove the file from the repo and history, and inject via a secrets manager (Vault/SSM/CI secrets).

findings.title: `!.env` negation force-un-ignores the secrets file
findings.message: The `!.env` line ('keep ours checked in for staging') is the mechanism that lands the committed secrets above. Staging config does not belong in git, least of all next to keys labeled `prod-2026` and `pg_live_`. Remove `!.env` and restore the default `.env` ignore. Having to fight the gitignore to land this file is the warning sign.

findings.title: FakeDb SQL-string parser with eval()-based WHERE engine is code injection
findings.message: FakeDb builds SQL strings from template literals (`WHERE email='${email}'`, `INSERT ... VALUES ('${email}'...)`) and `evalWhere` substitutes columns then calls `eval(expr)`. `listAllUsers(filter)` is fed the raw `req.query.filter` from admin endpoints, so an attacker can close a column ref and inject arbitrary JS (`email='x' || process.exit(1) || email='`). The `// Yes, we know.` comment ships it anyway. This entire class wraps a plain `UserRow[]` — delete it and replace with typed `Array.find`/`Array.filter` helpers and a column allow-list; no eval, no string SQL.

findings.title: new Function() on user-supplied filter string is remote code execution
findings.message: `new Function('tx', 'return (' + filterExpr + ')')` compiles `req.query.filter` into executable JS running in the server process — any authenticated admin (or anyone using the adminBypass/master-key bypass) can call `require`, read `process.env`, or exfiltrate data. Delete the flexible-filter feature (YAGNI with an RCE consequence); if filtering is genuinely needed, accept a structured filter object and implement only the operators required. (Note: it is also compiled once per row inside `.filter()`, but the fix is removal, so the perf angle is moot.)

findings.title: Hardcoded 'admin-master-key' bypasses JWT verification entirely
findings.message: `if (token === 'admin-master-key')` returns a full admin TokenPayload with no signature check — a permanent, unrotatable backdoor now visible in source on every deployed instance. Delete this branch. If dev/super-admin tooling is needed, issue a real signed JWT with its own secret.

findings.title: ?adminBypass=1 query param grants admin identity with no credential
findings.message: Appending `?adminBypass=1` to any route injects `{ email: 'admin@admin.com', role: 'admin' }` and calls next() — a full auth bypass dressed up as a 'staging debug' flag, and it's copy-pasted into all four AdminController methods too. Delete it. If a scoped test identity is truly needed, gate it on `NODE_ENV === 'test'` plus a non-guessable internal header.

findings.title: Full PAN and CVC stored on the transaction and console.log'd
findings.message: `card_number: input.card.number` and `cvc: input.card.cvc` are added to the persisted Transaction (`transactions.save(transaction)`), then `console.log('charge processed', JSON.stringify(transaction))` dumps both to stdout. This is a direct PCI-DSS violation (CVC must never be stored at all). Remove both fields; `cardLast4` already exists and is the only allowed derived value. Delete the console.log.

findings.title: Full card number exported as a CSV column
findings.message: `exportTransactionsCsv` emits `tx.card_number` as a column, so `GET /admin/export.csv` hands raw PANs to any admin. Even after the PAN-storage bug is fixed, remove this column — PCI-DSS forbids exporting full PANs in plaintext. Use `card_last4` only.

findings.title: console.log of raw register body logs plaintext passwords
findings.message: `console.log('register request:', JSON.stringify(req.body))` runs before hashing, dumping `{ email, password }` to stdout and any log aggregator. Delete the line; rely on the pino request logger and never log the body of an auth endpoint.

findings.title: password_hash returned in the register response body
findings.message: The 201 response includes `user.password_hash`. Even an MD5 hash is a credential / offline-cracking oracle and must not leave the server. Return only `{ id, email, role }` plus the token.

findings.title: MD5 used for password hashing
findings.message: `createHash('md5')` is an unsalted, work-factor-free general digest — the whole user table is crackable with rainbow tables in seconds if it leaks. Replace with bcrypt, scrypt (`node:crypto.scrypt`), or argon2. Single-function change, no API impact.

findings.title: Password reset token returned in HTTP response and console.log'd
findings.message: `res.json({ ok: true, resetToken })` hands the reset token straight back to the caller, defeating the entire point of a reset flow — anyone who can POST a victim's email gets the token. `console.log('password reset token for ...')` also leaks it to logs. The token must travel out-of-band (email) only; respond with `{ ok: true }` and remove the log.

findings.title: SSRF via unvalidated webhook URLs; payloads sent to every owner's hooks
findings.message: Any authenticated user can register a webhook to `http://169.254.169.254/...`, an RFC-1918 host, or a `file://`/`javascript:` URL (the controller only checks `typeof url === 'string'`), then trigger a charge to make the server fetch it; `redirect: 'follow'` enables redirect chains. Worse, `fireForTransaction` iterates ALL webhooks regardless of owner, leaking every transaction to every subscriber. Fix: validate at the boundary (https-only, reject link-local/private CIDRs, `z.string().url()`) AND filter hooks to the transaction's owner.

findings.title: JWTs issued with no expiry — valid forever
findings.message: `jwt.sign(payload, SECRET)` has no `expiresIn`, so a stolen token grants permanent access. Add `{ expiresIn: '1h' }` (per session policy) so credentials rotate and go stale.

findings.title: generateResetToken uses Math.random() (not a CSPRNG)
findings.message: `Math.random()` is a non-cryptographic PRNG; reset tokens built from it are predictable. Use `crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex')`. (Currently moot because the token is also returned in the response — fix both.)

findings.title: Admin email-domain check copy-pasted into all four handlers instead of requireAdmin
findings.message: `requireAdmin` already exists in authMiddleware.ts but each of listUsers/listTransactions/stats/exportCsv re-implements the `endsWith('@admin.com')` block (and re-adds the adminBypass escape). Wire `requireAdmin` as route middleware in routes.ts and delete the four inline copies so the admin policy lives in one place.

findings.title: cors({ origin: '*', credentials: true }) is a broken combination
findings.message: Browsers refuse to honor `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true` together with `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *`, so credentialed cross-origin requests silently fail while appearing permissive. Either drop `credentials: true` (true open CORS) or enumerate the actual allowed origins.

findings.title: JSON body limit raised 64kb -> 5mb globally is a DoS amplifier
findings.message: `express.json({ limit: '5mb' })` applies to every route, including /charge and the auth endpoints, letting an attacker force 5mb parses everywhere. The stated reason (CSV export) is a GET with no body. Keep the global limit at 64kb and scope any larger cap to the specific admin POST route that needs it.

findings.title: Login returns 404 for unknown email — user enumeration
findings.message: The 'no user with that email' (404) branch is distinguishable from the wrong-password (401) branch, letting an attacker enumerate registered emails. Return a single generic 401 'invalid credentials' for both cases.

findings.title: Auth endpoints read raw body with no validation schema
findings.message: `email`/`password` are pulled straight from `req.body` with no Zod schema (unlike the charge boundary). Empty-string passwords pass the `!password` guard, non-string emails are accepted, and there is no length cap (hash-of-10MB-blob DoS). Add `z.object({ email: z.string().email(), password: z.string().min(8).max(128) })`.

findings.title: Webhook fan-out awaited synchronously in the charge hot path
findings.message: `await this.webhooks.fireForTransaction(transaction)` runs before the charge response, so charge latency = gateway + sum of every subscriber's response time, and one hung subscriber stalls the payment. Enqueue delivery (job/queue keyed by transactionId) and return immediately; deliver asynchronously.

findings.title: @ts-nocheck disables type checking across userStore and adminService
findings.message: `// @ts-nocheck` tops both userStore.ts and adminService.ts — the credential store and the admin service — in an otherwise strict-TS codebase, silencing exactly the checker that would catch bugs in the unsafe `new Function`/`eval` code and the snake_case (`password_hash`, `created_at`) vs camelCase domain drift. Fix the underlying types (UserRow is defined right there) and delete the pragmas.

findings.title: Module-level `db` singleton leaks state across tests/requests
findings.message: `const db = new FakeDb()` at module scope means every createApp() shares one user table — auth.test.ts's two tests already write into the same store, creating latent order-dependency, and it can't be reset or doubled. Inject the store via constructor as the rest of the repo does.

findings.title: Module-level `webhooks` array is hidden global state
findings.message: `const webhooks: Webhook[] = []` at module scope is shared across all WebhooksService instances and all tests, so any registration contaminates later tests and can never be reset. Inject the array/repository via the constructor instead of closing over module state.

findings.title: Webhook delivery is serial — N sequential external round-trips
findings.message: The `for (const hook of webhooks)` loop awaits each `fetch()` before starting the next, so total time is N x RTT. Use `await Promise.allSettled(webhooks.map(hook => fetch(...)))` so deliveries run concurrently. (Lower priority than the hot-path and SSRF issues on the same method.)

findings.title: findAll() then in-memory filter — unbounded read per admin request
findings.message: `listAllTransactions` loads the entire transaction table then filters in process. Fine on seed data, but it won't scale when the repo is swapped for a real DB. Push predicates to the store and/or add a hard server-side LIMIT before this sees production volume.

findings.title: exportTransactionsCsv buffers the whole table in memory
findings.message: `findAll()` plus building the full CSV string holds the entire table (as objects and as a string) in memory at once, risking OOM on large datasets. Stream rows in batches through a CSV transform into the response. Same unbounded-read caveat applies to listUsers/stats. Forward-looking given the current in-memory store.

findings.title: Stray console.log calls bypass the pino logger (and its redaction)
findings.message: Beyond the security-relevant logs flagged separately, several debug `console.log`s remain (webhook firing/response/failure, and `userStore.findUserByEmail sql:` which prints the query + email). These skip pino's levels, structured fields, and redaction allowlist — the exact pipeline that would have caught the PAN/password leaks. Replace with `logger.debug/info/error({...}, 'msg')` or delete.

findings.title: Tests cover only happy paths with `toBeDefined()` assertions
findings.message: `expect(res.body.token).toBeDefined()` passes for almost any value and proves nothing about correctness, and the suite tests only two happy paths. Assert the real contract (status 201, token is a 3-part JWT string, response has no `password_hash`) and add the dangerous negative cases: duplicate email -> 409, wrong password -> 401 (not 404), /auth/me without token -> 401, /auth/me with ?adminBypass=1 -> rejected, non-admin -> 403 on /admin/users, and password-reset response without `resetToken`.
